refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the ability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The latter is a performance constraint on the system's operation, not a functional requirement of the system itself. The refined FR focuses on the functional capability—providing visibility into energy efficiency—which is solution-neutral and measurable. Achievement of the energy_per_ton ≤ E_target acceptance criterion is a system-level performance requirement that emerges from correct design of propulsion, hydraulics, and operational control DPs, not from a single FR.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the NGHE solution can capture energy input (kWh or equivalent) and work output (tons moved, distance, cycles) over a defined evaluation period, and compute energy_per_ton. Verify that reported metrics are traceable to instrumentation and that the calculation method is documented and repeatable.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure total energy consumed during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure or infer total work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or task cycles) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute and log the energy efficiency metric (energy per unit work output) at defined intervals.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline via data aggregation pipeline.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and work sensors in the mobile unit; compute energy_per_ton locally on-board; transmit periodic summaries to central reporting system.

DP3: Deploy independent energy metering at fuel/power distribution point and independent tonnage tracking via third-party logistics/weighing infrastructure; reconcile and compute ratio via external audit process.

DP4: Instrument energy consumption via non-invasive monitoring (e.g., power draw inference, fuel consumption modeling); instrument work output via GPS/odometry and load estimation; fuse data streams for ratio calculation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline via data aggregation pipeline.
selected_dp.rationale_for_selection: DP1 minimizes coupling between energy and work measurement subsystems by treating them as independent data sources that converge only at the computation layer. This supports modularity (separate IM boundaries for energy metering, work tracking, and analytics), reduces real-time constraints, and allows measurement methods to evolve independently. It aligns with acceptance criteria (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) by enabling rigorous post-evaluation analysis over agreed periods without requiring synchronized on-board computation.

potential_sources: Energy and work measurement instruments may share a common power supply or communication bus (mitigated by independent sensor design and redundant pathways).
Data aggregation pipeline depends on both data streams being available and synchronized (mitigated by explicit buffering and reconciliation logic in IM).
Offline computation introduces latency; if real-time feedback is later required, DP1 may need extension (acceptable as future evolution, not current coupling).
coupling_check.notes: DP1 exhibits low functional coupling: energy measurement is logically independent of work measurement. Both feed a stateless aggregation function. No feedback loops or circular dependencies detected. Comparison: DP2 couples sensors and computation on-board (higher complexity, tighter real-time constraints); DP3 introduces external audit dependency (reduces autonomy); DP4 couples inference models across domains (higher epistemic risk).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy source (fuel tank, power grid connection) is accessible for instrumentation without operational disruption. Risk: Retrofit constraints on existing equipment may force invasive installation. Mitigation: Conduct site survey; consider non-invasive alternatives (DP4) if access is limited.

Assumption: Work output (tonnage moved) can be measured at point of delivery with acceptable accuracy. Risk: Informal worksites may lack weighing infrastructure. Mitigation: Establish baseline measurement protocol; accept proxy metrics (e.g., load count, distance) if tonnage is unavailable, with documented conversion factors.

Assumption: Data aggregation pipeline can reliably collect, store, and process measurement streams over evaluation period. Risk: Data loss, clock skew, or communication gaps may invalidate ratio calculation. Mitigation: Implement redundant logging; enforce time-sync protocols; define data quality thresholds for acceptance.

Assumption: Energy_per_ton metric is stable and meaningful over the evaluation period (i.e., work output and energy consumption are not decoupled by external factors). Risk: Seasonal variation, equipment degradation, or operational changes may confound results. Mitigation: Stratify analysis by operational mode; document confounding variables; agree on normalization method with stakeholders.

Assumption: Acceptance criteria (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is achievable with current equipment and practices. Risk: Target may be unrealistic, leading to failed evaluation. Mitigation: Conduct baseline measurement before NGHE deployment; set E_target based on empirical data, not aspirational goals.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall enable continuous measurement and periodic reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton = total_energy_used / total_tonnage_moved) such that the ratio satisfies ≤ E_target over the agreed evaluation period, with measurement accuracy ±X% and data availability ≥Y%, and shall support post-evaluation audit and root-cause analysis of deviations. DP1 shall be decomposed into three independent sub-DPs: (DP1a) Measure energy input at source with specified accuracy and logging frequency; (DP1b) Measure work output at point of delivery with specified accuracy and logging frequency; (DP1c) Aggregate and compute ratio offline with defined reconciliation logic and error handling. Each sub-DP shall map to a distinct IM (energy metering module, work tracking module, analytics module) with clear interface contracts (data schema, timing, quality gates). This revision makes acceptance criteria testable and IM boundaries explicit.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level data aggregation, energy accounting, and efficiency computation pipeline that collects instrumented energy input and work output telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, performs offline ratio analysis, and reports aggregate efficiency metrics.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives instrumented energy consumption (fuel/electricity) and work output (tonnage moved) measurements from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules at regular intervals.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Optionally sends efficiency-based task recommendations or operational guidance back to Teleoperation based on aggregated ratio analysis.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Flags anomalous energy-to-work ratios that may indicate mechanical degradation or safety concerns requiring intervention.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the system-level integration layer responsible for fleet-wide data aggregation, analytics, and offline computation. Energy instrumentation logic and work output measurement belong at source (Vehicle), but aggregation, ratio computation, and efficiency reporting are naturally owned by NGHE. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry ingestion and alert/tasking egress.

Poor fit for the full DP. Vehicle owns energy input instrumentation and work output measurement at the point of generation, but offline aggregation and ratio computation across a fleet belong at system level, not in a single vehicle. Placing the pipeline here would require Vehicle to maintain state across multiple trips and diffuse efficiency logic across the fleet.

Weak fit. Teleoperation is a control/command interface, not a data aggregation or analytics layer. Placing offline pipeline logic here conflates operational control with fleet-level efficiency analysis. High diffusion: would require Teleoperation to manage persistent state and coordinate data from multiple vehicles.

Moderate-weak fit. Safety Services may consume efficiency alerts (e.g., degradation warnings), but the core responsibility for energy-to-work ratio computation and aggregation is not a safety function. Placing the pipeline here would blur safety-specific logic with general fleet analytics.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is best contained within NGHE Solution because it is fundamentally a system-level, offline analytics function that aggregates data from distributed sources (Vehicle energy/work instrumentation) and produces fleet-wide efficiency insights. NGHE Solution is the natural owner of cross-vehicle data pipelines, aggregation logic, and offline computation. Vehicle and Teleoperation provide the instrumentation and measurement points (which they own), but the aggregation and ratio computation belong at the system integration layer.
containment_risks: If Vehicle instrumentation is incomplete or inconsistent, the aggregation pipeline will produce unreliable ratios. Mitigation: establish clear contracts for telemetry schema and measurement accuracy at the Vehicle boundary.

Offline computation introduces latency; if real-time efficiency feedback is later required, the DP may need refactoring to support streaming computation. Mitigation: document the offline assumption and plan for future streaming variant if needed.
Data quality and missing samples from vehicles in the field may degrade aggregation. Mitigation: implement robust data validation and gap-handling logic within the pipeline.

If efficiency alerts must trigger immediate operational changes, the boundary between NGHE Solution (offline) and Teleoperation (real-time control) must be clearly defined. Mitigation: use SafetyAlert interface for critical anomalies; use Tasking for advisory recommendations.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly and sufficiently addresses the FR. The three-part strategy (instrument energy input at source, instrument work output at delivery point, compute ratio offline via aggregation pipeline) is logically sound and maps cleanly to the functional requirement to 'enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output.' The offline aggregation approach is appropriate for fleet-level efficiency analysis and aligns with the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over evaluation period).

diffusion_risks: Instrumentation responsibility split: Energy input instrumentation and work output measurement are owned by Vehicle module, while aggregation and ratio computation are owned by NGHE Solution. This is architecturally sound (measurement at source, analytics at system level), but requires robust telemetry contracts and schema versioning to prevent data quality degradation.

Offline computation latency: DP specifies offline ratio computation, which introduces delay between measurement and reporting. If operational feedback loops (Tasking interface) must respond in near-real-time, the offline assumption may become a bottleneck. Mitigation: document latency SLA and plan for streaming variant if real-time efficiency feedback becomes a future requirement.

Data completeness and gap handling: Fleet-wide aggregation depends on consistent telemetry from all vehicles. Missing or malformed samples from vehicles in the field will degrade ratio accuracy. Mitigation: implement explicit data validation, gap detection, and handling logic within the NGHE Solution pipeline; establish SLAs for telemetry availability.

Alert vs. advisory boundary: SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces both egress from NGHE Solution to Teleoperation/Safety Services. The distinction between critical anomalies (SafetyAlert) and advisory recommendations (Tasking) must be clearly defined to avoid conflating offline analytics with real-time safety control. Mitigation: define alert thresholds and escalation rules explicitly in interface contracts.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment fit (0.92 score). NGHE Solution is the correct owner of the offline aggregation pipeline, ratio computation, and fleet-level efficiency reporting. Vehicle owns energy and work instrumentation at the point of generation (0.45 containment for full DP is expected; Vehicle is not responsible for fleet-wide aggregation). Teleoperation (0.35) and Safety Services (0.4) are correctly excluded from pipeline ownership. The DP-to-IM mapping is clean: the core logic (ratio computation, aggregation) is localized to NGHE Solution; external dependencies (telemetry ingestion, alert/tasking egress) are minimal and well-defined via interfaces. Diffusion score of 0.08 is acceptable for a system-level integration function.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly measurable from the DP outputs. NGHE Solution will compute energy_per_ton as (total_energy_used / total_ton_moved) over the evaluation period. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation accuracy at Vehicle (energy and tonnage sensors), (2) data completeness and quality in the aggregation pipeline, (3) correct ratio computation logic. All are testable and auditable. Recommend establishing baseline E_target value and evaluation period duration in the worksite operational policies.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to NGHE Solution as the primary owner of measurement aggregation and reporting. Vehicle provides the instrumented inputs (energy, tonnage). Teleoperation and Safety Services consume outputs (efficiency recommendations, anomaly alerts). Ownership boundaries are explicit and non-overlapping. Recommend documenting telemetry schema, measurement units, and aggregation time windows in interface specifications to ensure end-to-end traceability from FR to IM.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP1 to explicitly state the offline aggregation time window (e.g., 'compute ratio offline via daily/weekly data aggregation pipeline') to clarify latency assumptions and support future streaming variants.

Add a sub-requirement or design note specifying telemetry schema contracts (energy units, tonnage units, timestamp precision, missing-data handling) to be owned by Vehicle and consumed by NGHE Solution. This reduces diffusion risk from data quality issues.

Define alert thresholds and escalation rules for SafetyAlert interface (e.g., 'flag energy_per_ton > 1.2 × E_target as anomaly requiring investigation'). This clarifies the boundary between offline analytics and real-time safety control.

Document the assumption that offline computation is acceptable for the current evaluation period. If future operational requirements demand real-time efficiency feedback, plan a DP variant that supports streaming computation within NGHE Solution or delegates real-time ratio checks to Vehicle/Teleoperation.
Establish acceptance criteria for data completeness (e.g., 'aggregation pipeline must include ≥95% of vehicle telemetry samples over evaluation period') to ensure ratio accuracy is verifiable.

decision.justification: Gate results confirm FR1 is well-formed, DP1 is logically sound and architecturally contained, and IM allocation is clear. However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks expose critical sub-requirements that must be decomposed to ensure end-to-end traceability and risk mitigation. Specifically: (1) telemetry schema contracts and data quality assurance are not yet explicitly owned or verified; (2) offline aggregation latency and time-window assumptions require sub-level design decisions; (3) alert threshold definition and SafetyAlert/Tasking boundary clarity are safety-critical and must be decomposed to prevent conflation of analytics with real-time control. High safety criticality tailoring factor reinforces the need to decompose these concerns into explicit sub-FRs before proceeding to detailed design. Stopping here would leave diffusion risks unresolved and verification criteria incomplete.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Enable reliable telemetry ingestion and schema validation for energy and work-output measurements from Vehicle module to NGHE Solution aggregation pipeline.
FR1.2: Compute and report energy-per-unit-work ratio with defined latency and time-window granularity (e.g., daily, weekly) to support offline efficiency analysis and future real-time variants.

FR1.3: Detect and flag energy efficiency anomalies (energy_per_ton > threshold) and escalate to SafetyAlert or Tasking interface with clear criticality classification to prevent conflation of analytics with real-time safety control.
FR1.4: Ensure data completeness and gap handling in fleet-wide aggregation pipeline to maintain ratio accuracy and verifiability against acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target).

revise.why: DP1 is logically sound but lacks explicit specification of: (a) telemetry schema contracts (units, precision, missing-data handling), (b) aggregation time window and latency SLA, (c) alert threshold definition and escalation rules. These refinements should be incorporated into DP1 or explicitly delegated to sub-level DPs (FR1.1–FR1.4) to reduce diffusion risk and clarify safety-critical boundaries. Recommend revising DP1 to reference these sub-level design decisions and add a note that offline computation is a current-state assumption subject to future streaming variants.

assumptions: Offline aggregation latency is acceptable for current evaluation period; real-time efficiency feedback is not a current operational requirement.
Vehicle module provides instrumented energy and work-output measurements with sufficient accuracy and completeness (≥95% sample availability).
Telemetry schema (units, precision, timestamp format, missing-data encoding) is stable and versioned across Vehicle and NGHE Solution.
SafetyAlert and Tasking interfaces have distinct thresholds and escalation rules; offline analytics do not directly trigger real-time safety interlocks.
NGHE Solution has sufficient computational and storage capacity to aggregate fleet-wide telemetry and compute ratios within defined time windows.
open_risks: Telemetry schema drift or version mismatch between Vehicle and NGHE Solution could degrade data quality and ratio accuracy. Mitigation: establish schema versioning and validation contracts in FR1.1.

Offline aggregation latency may become a bottleneck if operational feedback loops (Tasking interface) require near-real-time efficiency recommendations. Mitigation: document latency SLA in FR1.2 and plan streaming variant.

Data completeness failures (missing or malformed samples) will reduce ratio accuracy and verifiability. Mitigation: implement explicit gap detection and handling logic in FR1.4; establish telemetry availability SLAs.

Alert threshold definition (energy_per_ton > 1.2 × E_target) is currently informal. Unclear boundary between offline analytics (SafetyAlert) and real-time safety control (Tasking) could lead to conflation. Mitigation: decompose FR1.3 to define thresholds and escalation rules explicitly.

High safety criticality of efficiency monitoring (potential for unsafe work practices if efficiency feedback is delayed or inaccurate) requires rigorous verification of data quality and alert logic. Mitigation: establish acceptance criteria for data completeness and alert accuracy in FR1.4 and FR1.3.
evidence_needed: Telemetry schema specification (units, precision, timestamp format, missing-data encoding) agreed between Vehicle and NGHE Solution teams.
Aggregation time-window definition (daily, weekly, or other) and latency SLA documented in operational policies.
Alert threshold values (e.g., energy_per_ton > 1.2 × E_target) and escalation rules (SafetyAlert vs. Tasking) defined in interface contracts.
Data completeness acceptance criterion (e.g., ≥95% sample availability) and gap-handling logic specified in NGHE Solution design.
Baseline E_target value and evaluation period duration established in worksite operational policies.
Verification plan for ratio computation accuracy, including test cases for edge cases (missing data, sensor failures, schema version transitions).
